specification.topic-statement: The tool call formatting system renders tool invocations and their results into human-readable terminal text with structured argument display and content truncation.

scope.in-scope: Formatting tool call arguments from JSON into a key=value display, formatting tool results with type-sensitive extraction, content truncation, and the buffer management that supports incremental construction of formatted strings.
scope.boundary: This topic produces formatted strings. Rendering those strings to the terminal is outside this topic's scope.
This topic receives tool names, argument JSON strings, and result JSON strings. Executing tools or producing those values is outside this topic's scope.

field.name: arguments JSON
field.note: JSON object string; may be null or empty

field.name: result JSON
field.note: any JSON value; may be null

field.name: text
field.note: null-terminated string allocated under the caller-provided context

behavior.buffer-management: Formatted output is built incrementally using an internal byte-array buffer. The buffer supports printf-style formatted appends, raw string appends, and indentation appends (repeated space characters). The final string is extracted from the buffer as a null-terminated string; if the last byte already is a null terminator it is reused rather than appended again. The buffer length excludes any trailing null terminator.
behavior.formatting-a-tool-call:
A tool call is formatted as a single line of the form:
→ tool_name: arg1="value1", arg2=42, arg3=true
The line begins with the arrow prefix "→ " followed by the tool name and a colon and space.
If the arguments string is null or empty, only the prefix and tool name are produced. No colon, space, or argument list is appended.
If the arguments string is non-empty but cannot be parsed as JSON, the raw argument string is appended directly after a ": " separator, subject to the standard content truncation rules (three lines or 400 characters). No key=value formatting is applied.
If the arguments parse successfully as JSON but the root value is not a JSON object, the serialized JSON is appended after a ": " separator, subject to truncation. No key=value formatting is applied.
If the arguments are a valid JSON object with no fields, only the prefix and tool name are produced.
For a valid non-empty JSON object, each field is formatted as key=value and the fields are separated by ", ". The formatting of each value depends on its JSON type: - String values are surrounded by double quotes. - Number, boolean, and null values are rendered without quotes. - Array and object values are serialized back to compact JSON without quotes.
The complete argument list is produced without truncation.
behavior.formatting-a-tool-result:
A tool result is formatted as a single line of the form:
← tool_name: content
The line begins with the left-arrow prefix "← " followed by the tool name.
If the result string is null, the prefix and tool name are produced with no additional content.
If the result string is non-null but cannot be parsed as JSON, the raw result string is appended after a ": " separator, subject to truncation.
If the result parses as a JSON string, the string value (without JSON quotes) is extracted and appended after ": ", subject to truncation. If the extracted string is empty, the text "(no output)" is appended instead.
If the result parses as a JSON array, the array elements are joined with ", " and appended after ": ", subject to truncation. String elements in the array are appended directly (without JSON quotes); all other element types are serialized to compact JSON.
If the result parses as any other JSON type (object, number, boolean, null), the entire value is serialized to compact JSON and appended after ": ", subject to truncation.
If, after type dispatch, no content string was produced, the text "(no output)" is appended after ": ".
behavior.content-truncation:
Content is truncated before being appended if it exceeds either of two limits: more than three lines, or more than 400 characters (whichever limit is reached first). Scanning stops at the first byte that would violate either limit.
If the content is empty, the text "(no output)" is appended.
If truncation occurs, "..." is appended after the truncated content.
If truncation does not occur, the full content is appended as-is.
